errors: The implementation does not specify any clinically relevant parameters. It lacks age thresholds, look‑back time intervals, and value sets for conditions or medications, which are essential for a meaningful clinical decision support rule. Without these specifications the rule cannot be evaluated for clinical reasonableness.

Age thresholds (≥16 and <24) are appropriate for chlamydia screening per CDC/CPG, and a 1‑year look‑back interval aligns with annual screening recommendations. However, the “Reason for not performing Chlamydia Screening” value set is listed as “TBD” (to be determined), meaning it has not been defined. This omission does not match the clinical practice guideline, which requires a defined set of coded reasons (e.g., patient declined, already screened, not sexually active, etc.). The missing value set constitutes a mismatch.

; Age threshold is set to ≥ 50 years, but current colorectal cancer screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF 2021) recommend starting at age 45.; Colonoscopy look‑back interval is defined as 1 month, whereas guideline‑based decision support should consider a colonoscopy performed within the past 10 years (or the appropriate interval based on findings) to determine eligibility for repeat screening.
The lookback period of **1 month** for assessment is clinically unreasonable for prostate cancer screening; standard guidelines

Age threshold is set to ≥ 25 years, but most hypertension screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF, ACC/AHA) recommend initiating screening at age 18 years (or even 20 years). The 25‑year minimum is higher than the guideline‑based recommendation, creating a clinical mismatch. The 6‑month look‑back interval and the referenced value set OID appear appropriate.

; The “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” entry lists **190 mg/dL**, which is a cholesterol concentration, not a temporal interval. A look‑back period should be expressed as a duration (e.g., “1 year” or “12 months”), so this value is mismatched.; The age thresholds (≥ 30 years and ≤ 80 years) do not align with the typical statin‑therapy recommendations in most CPGs, which usually target adults **≥ 40 years** (or ≥ 21 years for certain risk groups) and often set an upper limit around **75 years** (or consider

The “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” entry lists **“9 g%”**, which is a laboratory value (HbA1c concentration) rather than a temporal interval. A look‑back period should be expressed in time units (e.g., 12 months, 1 year). This mismatch makes the implementation clinically unreasonable. The age threshold and value set identifier are appropriate.

; **Age lower bound** is set to ≥ 60 years, which is higher than current guideline recommendations for average‑risk colorectal cancer screening (USPSTF: start at age 45 years; many societies: start at age 50 years).; **Look‑back period** of 2 years for “Has Recent Colorectal Screening” does not accommodate the recommended colonoscopy interval of up to 10 years (or FIT annually). A 2‑year window may miss patients who are up‑to‑date with colonoscopy but have not been screened within the past 2 years.

Age threshold set to “Age ≥ 30 years” is inconsistent with standard HIV screening guidelines, which recommend routine screening for individuals starting at age 13–15 (or at least age 13) rather than age 30. This threshold is therefore clinically unreasonable.

The “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” section incorrectly includes a BMI threshold (35 kg/m²) as a look‑back period. BMI is a value threshold, not a temporal interval. The other values (age ≥ 35 years, age ≤ 70 years, 1‑year look‑back for recent glucose test, and the specified value set OIDs) are clinically appropriate.

The age lower bound is set to ≥ 30 years, but current cervical cancer screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF) recommend screening start at ≥ 21 years. The implementation therefore excludes the 21‑29 year age group, which is clinically unreasonable. All other values (upper age limit ≤ 65, 3‑year Pap‑only lookback, and the Pap test value set) are appropriate.

Age threshold is set to ≥ 25 years, which is higher than the guideline‑recommended starting age for alcohol misuse screening (typically ≥ 18 years for adults). The lookback period (1 year) and the value set OID for Alcohol Use Screening are appropriate.

The lookback period for “Has Recent DEXA Scan” is set to 6 months, which is clinically too short. Current guidelines recommend repeating a DEXA scan no more frequently than every 2 years (unless there is a specific clinical indication). All other thresholds and value sets appear appropriate.

Age lower bound is set to ≥ 55 years, but guideline‑recommended screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm is typically for patients **≥ 65 years** (often 65–75 years) who have a history of tobacco use. The upper bound of ≤ 75 years is appropriate. No other mismatches were identified.
